masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through College Football, Vol. 2
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through College Football, Vol. 2
masthead.dek: A fresh round on the college game — programs and titles, the Heisman and great players, and the bowls, rivalries, and traditions. Every answer routes to a school, a count, or a trophy, reached by reasoning. Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Built fresh against the existing College Football set. Verified to the NCAA, Sports Reference, Heisman.com, and ESPN.

dimensions: national titles by a faded power
title count of a single dynasty
first SEC charter member to leave
pre-WWII Big Ten dynasty title run
league that fed the Big 12 merger
conference Notre Dame joined for non-football sports
most wins at one school by a coach
ACC founder that left for the Big Ten
Big Ten charter member that left permanently
eighth school added to the new ACC
SEC founder that became an ACC member
decade of the first BCS title game
school pushed to most Heismans by a reinstated trophy
Heisman winner who coached a Heisman winner
school of single-season rushing-yards record holder
school of career passing-yards record holder
position of first sophomore Heisman winner
school with most recent Heisman runner-ups without a win
school's first-ever Heisman winner taken No. 1 in 2026
school of single-season passing records holder
Tebow's count of Heisman finalist invitations
count of Heisman winners who were Super Bowl MVPs
school of career rushing-touchdowns record holder
school of first freshman Heisman winner
Heisman QB who won a 2025 high-school state title
school of two-time Heisman runner-up Leroy Keyes
rivalry trophy name vs. game nickname
plan.note: Three branches (programs/titles/conferences, Heisman/players/records, bowls/rivalries/traditions), each with a reflex-defeater, fresh dimensions distinct from the first 40 questions.

questions.cat: College football · most NCAA-recognized national titles
questions.q: If you count every national title the NCAA officially recognizes, no modern football power tops the list. Which old Ivy League school owns the most NCAA-recognized national championships?
questions.detail: Yale holds 18 NCAA-recognized national championships, more than any other program, nearly all from football's 19th-century dawn.
questions.why: The reflex is a current dynasty like Alabama (16) or a blueblood like Notre Dame; people forget the NCAA's own record book credits the Ivy founders, and Yale leads it.
questions.more: Princeton is right behind with 15 recognized; both schools dominated the sport before forward passing reshaped it.
questions.source: NCAA.com: College football teams with the most national championships (2025-01-23); ESPN, 'Who has won the most college football championships?'

questions.cat: College football · most self-claimed national titles
questions.q: One program claims 28 national titles all its own, far more than any school you'd name today, though the NCAA only recognizes about half. Which school makes that claim?
questions.detail: Princeton claims 28 national championships, many awarded retroactively by historical selectors; the NCAA recognizes 15.
questions.why: Asking for 'claimed' rather than 'recognized' flips the leaderboard away from modern powers; the gaudy 28 belongs to a school nobody thinks of as a titan now.
questions.more: The gap between claimed and recognized is why title counts are so contested for the sport's earliest era.
questions.source: ESPN, 'Who has won the most college football championships?'; CFP Bubble Watch, 'National Titles by School All-Time'

questions.cat: College football · national titles by a faded power
questions.dimension: national titles by a faded power
questions.q: Among programs still chasing modern glory, one northeastern school quietly claims nine national titles won under three different coaches, the last way back in 1976. Which school?
questions.detail: Pitt claims nine national championships: three under Pop Warner, five under Jock Sutherland, and one under Johnny Majors in 1976.
questions.why: Nine titles sounds like a current blueblood, but Pitt's haul is mostly pre-WWII; the slicer 'last in 1976' rules out every program people associate with dynasties today.
questions.more: Sutherland actually played for Warner at Pitt before stacking five titles of his own as head coach.
questions.source: University of Pittsburgh, 'Pitt Football: Through the Years'; Britannica, 'Jock Sutherland'

questions.cat: College football · title count of a single dynasty
questions.dimension: title count of a single dynasty
questions.q: The school in south Florida built a 1980s-and-'90s run that defined an era, winning national titles in five different seasons. How many national championships does that program claim?
questions.detail: Miami (FL) claims five national championships: 1983, 1987, 1989, 1991, and 2001.
questions.why: Fans rattle off 'four' because the run feels like four iconic teams, but the 2001 squad pushes the real count to five; the year span itself is the defeater.
questions.more: All five came in a tight 18-year window, one of the most concentrated dynasties in the sport's history.
questions.source: University of Miami Athletics, 'National Championships'; Sports-Reference, Miami (FL) Hurricanes

questions.cat: College football · first SEC charter member to leave
questions.dimension: first SEC charter member to leave
questions.q: When the SEC kicked off play in 1933, it had thirteen members. Which tiny school, with fewer than 300 students at the time, was the first charter member to quit the league, leaving in 1940?
questions.detail: Sewanee (University of the South) resigned from the SEC in 1940 after going 0-37 in conference play, the first original member to depart.
questions.why: People guess Georgia Tech or Tulane, the famous departures; both left decades later. The first to bail was a school most fans don't know ever belonged.
questions.more: Georgia Tech left in 1964 and Tulane in 1965, long after Sewanee's quiet exit.
questions.source: Saturday Down South, 'A brief history of the Southeastern Conference'; Wikipedia, 'Southeastern Conference'

questions.cat: College football · pre-WWII Big Ten dynasty title run
questions.dimension: pre-WWII Big Ten dynasty title run
questions.q: A Big Ten program ran off five national titles in eight years during the 1930s and early '40s under one coach, then never won another. Which school had that forgotten dynasty?
questions.detail: Minnesota won five national championships (1934, 1935, 1936, 1940, 1941) under Bernie Bierman, then never claimed another.
questions.why: Today Minnesota is a Big Ten middleweight, so a five-title dynasty reads as impossible; the 1930s setting is the only clue that points away from modern powers.
questions.more: Bierman's Gophers also posted four perfect seasons in that span.
questions.source: University of Minnesota Athletics, 'Bernie Bierman'; Wikipedia, 'Minnesota Golden Gophers football under Bernie Bierman'

questions.cat: College football · ACC charter member that departed
questions.q: The ACC was founded in 1953 by seven schools breaking off from a bigger southern league. One of those seven charter members later bolted the conference entirely in 1971. Which charter member left?
questions.detail: South Carolina was one of the ACC's seven 1953 charter members but withdrew in 1971, eventually landing in the SEC in 1992.
questions.why: People assume every founder stayed put, or they name Maryland (which left in 2014); the original defector was South Carolina, decades earlier.
questions.more: The other six founders were Clemson, Duke, Maryland, North Carolina, NC State, and Wake Forest; Virginia joined months later.
questions.source: Clemson University, 'The Atlantic Coast Conference'; Wikipedia, 'Atlantic Coast Conference'

questions.cat: College football · league that fed the Big 12 merger
questions.dimension: league that fed the Big 12 merger
questions.q: The Big 12 launched its first football season in 1996, born from a merger. Which now-defunct league supplied four of its schools, while a whole other conference folded into it?
questions.detail: The Big 12 formed in 1996 by merging the entire Big Eight with four Southwest Conference schools: Baylor, Texas, Texas A&M, and Texas Tech.
questions.why: Many recall the Big Eight half but blank on the dissolving league; the SWC name has faded from memory even though it died to make the Big 12.
questions.more: The conference's first game saw Kansas State beat Texas Tech 21-14 on August 31, 1996.
questions.source: Wikipedia, '1996 NCAA conference realignment'; SI, 'Big 12 expansion oral history'

questions.cat: College football · schools left after Pac-12 collapse
questions.q: When realignment gutted the Pac-12 in 2024, the league was left with just two schools that hadn't bolted. Name either of the two programs that remained.
questions.detail: After 10 members departed for the Big Ten, Big 12, and ACC in 2024, only Oregon State and Washington State stayed in the Pac-12.
questions.why: The famous names all fled to power leagues, so people forget the two that were stranded; the survivors were the league's smaller-market holdouts.
questions.more: The pair scheduled 2024 football games with the Mountain West while planning to rebuild the conference.
questions.source: CBS Sports, 'Oregon State, Washington State reach settlement with Pac-12 departing members'; ESPN, 'The Pac-12 leftovers'

questions.cat: College football · conference Notre Dame joined for non-foot
questions.dimension: conference Notre Dame joined for non-football sports
questions.q: Notre Dame famously stays independent in football. But the school does belong to one conference for almost all its other sports, and even played a full football schedule in it during 2020. Which league?
questions.detail: Notre Dame joined the ACC in 2013 for most sports while keeping football independent, but played a full ACC football slate in the 2020 COVID season.
questions.why: The reflex is 'Notre Dame has no conference,' which is only true for football; the school is an ACC member everywhere else, and briefly there in football too.
questions.more: The deal commits the Irish to five ACC football opponents most years.
questions.source: Notre Dame News, 'Notre Dame to join Atlantic Coast Conference'; Yahoo Sports, 'What conference is Notre Dame in?'

questions.cat: College football · first conference championship game
questions.q: In 1992 one league became the first in major college football to stage a postseason title game pitting two division winners. Which conference held that trailblazing championship game?
questions.detail: The SEC played the first FBS conference championship game in 1992 at Birmingham's Legion Field after expanding to 12 teams and splitting into divisions.
questions.why: The format is now everywhere, so it feels timeless; people forget one league invented it, and that the inaugural game predates the Big 12 or anyone copying it.
questions.more: Alabama beat Florida 28-21 in that first title game en route to a national championship.
questions.source: NOLA.com, 'How the first SEC championship game changed college football'; Wikipedia, 'SEC Championship Game'

questions.cat: College football · most wins at one school by a coach
questions.dimension: most wins at one school by a coach
questions.q: No coach in college football history won more games at a single school than this man, who spent 56 seasons at one program and finished with 408 victories. Which school did he coach the whole time?
questions.detail: Eddie Robinson won 408 games over 56 seasons, all at Grambling State, the record for victories at one institution.
questions.why: Big-name coaches changed jobs; Robinson's whole legend lives at one HBCU, so routing the answer to the school surprises those who only know power-conference names.
questions.more: His 408 wins led all of college football until 2003.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Eddie Robinson'; NOLA.com, 'Grambling icon Eddie Robinson coached final game'

questions.cat: College football · all-divisions career wins record
questions.q: Count every level of NCAA football and the all-time wins leader isn't a big-school legend at all. How many career wins did the small-college coach atop that list finish with, to the nearest ten?
questions.detail: John Gagliardi of Division III St. John's (MN) retired with 489 wins, the most of any coach across all NCAA divisions.
questions.why: People expect a famous FBS name and a number near 400; the real record sits well above that, set by a coach almost no casual fan can name.
questions.more: He coached for 60 years and finished 489-138-11.
questions.source: Fox News, 'All-time college football wins leader retires'; SI, 'Deion Sanders Bests Eddie Robinson's Early SWAC Record'

questions.cat: College football · weeks ranked AP No. 1
questions.q: Through the AP poll era no program has spent more total weeks ranked No. 1 than the all-time leader. Counting only the school in second place on that list, which Big Ten program is it?
questions.detail: Ohio State has spent the second-most weeks ranked AP No. 1 in history (about 120), trailing only Alabama (about 141).
questions.why: The slicer 'second place, Big Ten' filters out Alabama and Oklahoma and Notre Dame; reasoning a player toward the runner-up lands on a specific midwestern blueblood.
questions.more: Oklahoma and Notre Dame round out the next two spots on the all-time list.
questions.source: Winsipedia, 'College football weeks at AP No. 1'; Wikipedia, 'List of college football teams by weekly appearances atop AP poll'

questions.cat: College football · wire-to-wire AP No. 1 before 1950
questions.q: Before preseason polls began, two teams had finished a season ranked No. 1 from wire to wire. One was the service academy that did it in 1945. Which school?
questions.detail: Army was the AP's wire-to-wire No. 1 in 1945, one of only two teams (with Notre Dame in 1943) to do so before the 1950 preseason-poll era.
questions.why: Army as a national-title juggernaut is forgotten today, so the WWII-era dominance reads as a trick; the service-academy angle is the tell.
questions.more: Florida State became the first wire-to-wire No. 1 of the preseason-poll era in 1999.
questions.source: FightingIrish.com, 'CFP Championship Game – A Historical Perspective'; College Poll Archive, 'AP National Championships'

questions.cat: College football · second-most all-time bowl wins
questions.q: Alabama owns the most bowl-game victories in history. But which school sits second on that all-time list, with 38 bowl wins, despite playing in the SEC East's shadow for years?
questions.detail: Georgia ranks second all-time with 38 bowl wins, behind Alabama's 45, edging USC's 36.
questions.why: People jump to USC or a Rose Bowl regular; the runner-up is an SEC school whose steady bowl presence over decades quietly stacked wins.
questions.more: USC is just behind Georgia, making the top three a tight cluster.
questions.source: Saturday Down South, 'Which teams have won the most bowl games'; ESPN, 'College football bowl game history'

questions.cat: College football · ACC founder that left for the Big Ten
questions.dimension: ACC founder that left for the Big Ten
questions.q: In 2014 the Big Ten reached east for the first time, adding two schools and growing to 14. One had been an ACC charter member since 1953. Which school left the ACC for the Big Ten that year?
questions.detail: Maryland, an ACC charter member from 1953, left for the Big Ten in 2014 alongside Rutgers, pushing the league to 14 teams.
questions.why: Rutgers is the easy guess for the eastward move, but the school that abandoned a conference it helped found was Maryland; the '1953 charter' detail is the key.
questions.more: The two additions gave the Big Ten footholds in the Washington and New York media markets.
questions.source: CBS News, 'Maryland leaving ACC to join Big Ten in 2014'; Big Ten, 'Expansion History'

questions.cat: College football · Big Ten charter member that left permanen
questions.dimension: Big Ten charter member that left permanently
questions.q: Among the original Big Ten members from 1896, two early powers each later left the league for a stretch and then either rejoined or vanished. Which charter member quit football's biggest old league for good in 1946?
questions.detail: Chicago, a 1896 Big Ten charter member and early powerhouse, dropped football and withdrew from the conference in 1946, never to return.
questions.why: Michigan briefly left and came back; Chicago left and stayed gone, a fact that surprises anyone who pictures the Big Ten as eternally stable.
questions.more: Five founders (Illinois, Minnesota, Northwestern, Purdue, Wisconsin) have held continuous membership since 1896.
questions.source: Off Tackle Empire, 'The History of the Big Ten Part I'; Wikipedia, 'Big Ten Conference'

questions.cat: College football · decades with a national title
questions.q: Look only at schools that have won a recognized national title in the most different decades. How many distinct decades has Alabama claimed at least one championship in?
questions.detail: Alabama has won recognized national championships in seven different decades, a span matched among the very few longest-running powers.
questions.why: People sense Alabama is old but underestimate the decade count; 'seven' surprises because it stretches from the 1920s into the 2020s.
questions.more: Notre Dame is positioned to join Alabama at seven decades, underscoring how rare the feat is.
questions.source: Sportskeeda, '4 teams with more than 3 nattys in the last 50 seasons'; ESPN, 'Who has won the most college football championships?'

questions.cat: College football · eighth school added to the new ACC
questions.dimension: eighth school added to the new ACC
questions.q: The ACC began with seven charter members in 1953 but added an eighth school months later, before the league's first full year. Which state university joined as that eighth member in December 1953?
questions.detail: Virginia was admitted to the ACC in December 1953, becoming the eighth member after the original seven founded the league that May.
questions.why: People assume the famous seven were the whole start; the quick eighth addition is a detail that filters out anyone guessing from the charter list.
questions.more: The seven founders were Clemson, Duke, Maryland, UNC, NC State, South Carolina, and Wake Forest.
questions.source: Clemson University, 'The Atlantic Coast Conference'; ACC, 'Atlantic Coast Conference history'

questions.cat: College football · SEC founder that became an ACC member
questions.dimension: SEC founder that became an ACC member
questions.q: Two of the SEC's thirteen founders later left for other leagues. One quit in 1964 and spent years as an independent before eventually joining the ACC. Which school was that?
questions.detail: Georgia Tech, an SEC charter member, left the league in 1964, played as an independent, and later joined the ACC in 1979.
questions.why: Sewanee and Tulane are the usual 'who left the SEC' answers; the school that left and resurfaced in the ACC is a different founder entirely.
questions.more: Tulane, the other departing founder, left in 1965 and did not join a power conference.
questions.source: Saturday Down South, 'A brief history of the Southeastern Conference'; Wikipedia, 'Southeastern Conference'

questions.cat: College football · Big 12 original division alignment
questions.q: When the Big 12 began in 1996, it split into North and South divisions. Which division held the Texas schools plus Oklahoma and Oklahoma State?
questions.detail: The Big 12 South originally held Baylor, Oklahoma, Oklahoma State, Texas, Texas A&M, and Texas Tech; the North held the former Big Eight northern schools.
questions.why: It's easy to second-guess which way the Oklahoma schools went; pairing them with Texas in the South requires recalling the actual geography of the merger.
questions.more: The North was Colorado, Iowa State, Kansas, Kansas State, Missouri, and Nebraska.
questions.source: Wikipedia, '1996 NCAA conference realignment'; Heartland College Sports, 'Big 12 Football History: 1996 Season'

questions.cat: College football · decade of the first BCS title game
questions.dimension: decade of the first BCS title game
questions.q: Tennessee won the very first BCS national championship by beating its title-game opponent in the Fiesta Bowl. In which decade did that inaugural BCS title game take place?
questions.detail: The first BCS National Championship game was played for the 1998 season (January 1999), making it a 1990s contest.
questions.why: The BCS feels like a 2000s institution, so people misplace its debut; the first game actually closed out the 1990s.
questions.more: Tennessee beat Florida State 23-16 to finish 13-0.
questions.source: Wikipedia, '1998 Tennessee Volunteers football team'; UTSports.com, 'Remembering the 1998 National Champions'

questions.cat: College football · defensive Heisman winner's school
questions.q: Setting aside two-way and special-teams stars, only one player who spent most of his time on defense has ever won the Heisman Trophy. What school did he play for?
questions.detail: Cornerback Charles Woodson of Michigan won the 1997 Heisman, beating Tennessee's Peyton Manning, as the only primarily defensive player to win the award.
questions.why: A walkable set of decades of winners is overwhelmingly QBs and RBs; the reflex is to name an offense school. The slicer 'mostly defense' isolates a single player and routes the answer to his school.
questions.more: Woodson also returned punts and caught passes, but his fame came at cornerback.
questions.source: Heisman.com (Charles Woodson); Washington Post (2024)

questions.cat: College football · school pushed to most Heismans by a reins
questions.dimension: school pushed to most Heismans by a reinstated trophy
questions.q: One school has produced more Heisman Trophy winners than any other. What pushed it into sole first place was a 2024 decision to return a trophy it had once given back. Name the school.
questions.detail: When the Heisman Trust reinstated Reggie Bush's 2005 trophy in 2024, USC moved to eight winners, one ahead of Ohio State, Notre Dame, and Oklahoma at seven each.
questions.why: Several blue-bloods sit near the top, so the reflex picks Notre Dame or Ohio State. The clue about a returned, then reinstated trophy points only to USC and Bush.
questions.more: Bush had forfeited the award in 2010 over improper-benefits findings.
questions.source: FOX Sports (Heisman winners by school, 2024); ESPN (Reggie Bush reinstatement)

questions.cat: College football · Heisman winner who coached a Heisman winn
questions.dimension: Heisman winner who coached a Heisman winner
questions.q: Only one person has both won the Heisman Trophy and later coached a Heisman Trophy winner. Who is he?
questions.detail: Steve Spurrier won the 1966 Heisman at Florida, then as Florida's head coach guided Danny Wuerffel to the 1996 Heisman, the only person to do both.
questions.why: Many Heisman winners became coaches, so the set feels large, but the double condition of winning and coaching a winner leaves exactly one name.
questions.more: Spurrier built Florida's 'Fun 'n' Gun' offense around Wuerffel.
questions.source: Heisman.com (Catching Up With Our Heisman Winners In The Coaching Ranks); Heisman.com (Steve Spurrier)

questions.cat: College football · position shut out before 2015 Heisman
questions.q: Through 2015 the Heisman Trophy went to five straight quarterbacks before a runner from Alabama broke the streak. What position had been shut out during that run?
questions.detail: After Mark Ingram won in 2009, five straight quarterbacks took the Heisman until Derrick Henry of Alabama won in 2015 as a running back.
questions.why: The position set is tiny, but the reflex is to assume a back wins the Heisman often. The clue spotlights how long the trophy snubbed the position before Henry.
questions.more: Henry was only the second running back to win in sixteen years.
questions.source: Heisman.com (Derrick Henry); Bleacher Nation

questions.cat: College football · school of single-season rushing-yards rec
questions.dimension: school of single-season rushing-yards record holder
questions.q: One running back's single-season rushing total has stood as the major-college record since 1988 and may never be touched. What school did he play for?
questions.detail: Barry Sanders ran for 2,628 yards in the 1988 regular season at Oklahoma State, a record that still stands, and won the Heisman that year.
questions.why: Players will list famous backs from Texas, USC, or Alabama; the 1988-record slicer points to Sanders and his less-obvious school.
questions.more: He averaged nearly 239 rushing yards per game that season.
questions.source: Heisman.com (Barry Sanders); ESPN (Sanders' record-setting 1988 season)

questions.cat: College football · school of career passing-yards record hol
questions.dimension: school of career passing-yards record holder
questions.q: The all-time major-college leader in career passing yards never played for a powerhouse; he threw for over 19,000 yards at a Texas school. Name the school.
questions.detail: Case Keenum passed for 19,217 yards at Houston, the FBS career record, and is the only player with three different 5,000-yard seasons.
questions.why: The reflex is to name a blue-blood QB factory, but the record belongs to a Group-of-Five school. The set of high-volume passing programs is walkable to Houston.
questions.more: Keenum also holds the career touchdown-passes record at 155.
questions.source: ProFootballNetwork (Career Passing Yards Leaders); University of Houston Athletics

questions.cat: College football · position of first sophomore Heisman winne
questions.dimension: position of first sophomore Heisman winner
questions.q: In 2007 the Heisman Trophy was won for the first time by a player still in his second college season. What position did that breakthrough winner play?
questions.detail: Tim Tebow of Florida became the first sophomore to win the Heisman in 2007, doing it as a quarterback.
questions.why: The position set is small, but tying it to the 'first sophomore' milestone forces the solver to recall who that was rather than guess by frequency.
questions.more: Two more sophomores won the next two years after Tebow opened the door.
questions.source: Heisman.com (voting history); Sportskeeda (2007 finalists)

questions.cat: College football · school with most recent Heisman runner-up
questions.dimension: school with most recent Heisman runner-ups without a win
questions.q: One school had its players finish as Heisman runner-up four times in seven seasons without ever winning, including Andrew Luck twice and Christian McCaffrey. Name the school.
questions.detail: Stanford players finished second in Heisman voting four times between 2009 and 2015 (Toby Gerhart, Andrew Luck twice, Christian McCaffrey) without a winner.
questions.why: The reflex names a winning program; the 'runner-up but never won' slicer points to a school famous for near-misses, narrowing a walkable set to Stanford.
questions.more: Luck lost to Cam Newton in 2010 and Robert Griffin III in 2011.
questions.source: BroBible (most runner-up finishes); CBS Sports (2015 voting)

questions.cat: College football · school's first-ever Heisman winner taken
questions.dimension: school's first-ever Heisman winner taken No. 1 in 2026
questions.q: In 2026 a quarterback became the very first Heisman Trophy winner ever produced by his Big Ten school, then went first overall in the NFL draft. Name the school.
questions.detail: Fernando Mendoza won the Heisman for Indiana, the program's first, and the Raiders took him first overall in the 2026 NFL draft.
questions.why: The Big Ten's Heisman history points solvers to Michigan, Ohio State, or Wisconsin; the 'first ever for the school' clue lands on an unexpected program.
questions.more: He joined a short list of players to win the Heisman and go No. 1 the next spring.
questions.source: NCAA.com (Mendoza No. 1 overall, 2026); NFL.com

questions.cat: College football · school of single-season passing records h
questions.dimension: school of single-season passing records holder
questions.q: The single-season records for both passing yards and touchdown passes in major-college football were set in 2021 by a quarterback at a Conference USA school. Name the school.
questions.detail: Bailey Zappe of Western Kentucky threw for 5,967 yards and 62 touchdowns in 2021, breaking the FBS single-season marks held by B.J. Symons and Joe Burrow.
questions.why: The reflex picks a famous passing school; the records actually belong to a mid-major, and the set of record-chasing offenses is walkable to Western Kentucky.
questions.more: Zappe broke Burrow's touchdown record from LSU's 2019 title run.
questions.source: NCAA.com (Zappe breaks FBS records, 2021); ESPN

questions.cat: College football · Tebow's count of Heisman finalist invitat
questions.dimension: Tebow's count of Heisman finalist invitations
questions.q: How many times in his career did Tim Tebow get invited to New York as a Heisman finalist, a number no player had reached before him?
questions.detail: Tim Tebow was the first player invited to the Heisman ceremony as a finalist three times, winning once in 2007 and finishing as a finalist in 2008 and 2009.
questions.why: The count is a small, walkable set; the reflex underestimates how often Tebow was a finalist after his one win, making three a defensible-but-uncertain bet.
questions.more: Several players have since matched the three top-five finishes mark.
questions.source: ESPN (positions with most Heismans / finalist history); NCAA.com

questions.cat: College football · count of Heisman winners who were Super B
questions.dimension: count of Heisman winners who were Super Bowl MVPs
questions.q: Four players have won both the Heisman Trophy and a Super Bowl MVP award. How many is that exclusive group?
questions.detail: Roger Staubach, Jim Plunkett, Marcus Allen, and Desmond Howard are the only four to win both the Heisman and a Super Bowl MVP.
questions.why: The count is the answer; the reflex assumes many star college players reached this double, but the overlap is tiny and walkable.
questions.more: Howard won his Super Bowl MVP as a kick returner, not on offense.
questions.source: Heisman.com (A History of Heisman Winners in the Super Bowl); Pro Football Network

questions.cat: College football · school of career rushing-touchdowns recor
questions.dimension: school of career rushing-touchdowns record holder
questions.q: A small-school quarterback holds the major-college record for most career rushing touchdowns. What military academy did he play for?
questions.detail: Keenan Reynolds of Navy scored 88 rushing touchdowns from 2012 to 2015, the FBS career record, running the option offense.
questions.why: The reflex names a famous goal-line back, but the rushing-TD record belongs to an option quarterback, and the academy clue narrows the set to Navy.
questions.more: His total tops every running back in the record book.
questions.source: ProFootballNetwork (Career Rushing Touchdowns); Sports-Reference (CFB rushing records)

questions.cat: College football · school's first Heisman winner in 2009
questions.q: Mark Ingram won the 2009 Heisman Trophy, the first ever for his program despite its many titles. Name the school.
questions.detail: Mark Ingram won Alabama's first Heisman Trophy in 2009, surprising given the program's long history of national championships.
questions.why: The reflex assumes a storied champion already had a Heisman; the clue that 2009 was the school's first narrows to Alabama, which had none until Ingram.
questions.more: Alabama added more winners in the years that followed.
questions.source: Heisman.com (Mark Ingram); Bleacher Nation

questions.cat: College football · school of first freshman Heisman winner
questions.dimension: school of first freshman Heisman winner
questions.q: In 2012 the Heisman Trophy went for the first time to a player in his very first year of college eligibility. What school did this winner play for?
questions.detail: Johnny Manziel of Texas A&M won the 2012 Heisman as the first freshman ever to take the award.
questions.why: Solvers recall freshmen winning recently but blur the names; the 'first freshman' milestone fingerprints Manziel and routes to Texas A&M.
questions.more: He was the second-youngest winner ever at the time.
questions.source: ESPN (Manziel's Heisman win is first of its kind); NFL.com

questions.cat: College football · Heisman QB who won a 2025 high-school sta
questions.dimension: Heisman QB who won a 2025 high-school state title
questions.q: One former Heisman Trophy quarterback won his award at USC, then in 2025 coached his old high school to a California state title in his debut season. Who is he?
questions.detail: Carson Palmer won the 2002 Heisman at USC and, coaching his alma mater Santa Margarita, won a 2025 California state championship in his first season.
questions.why: The set of recognizable Heisman QBs is large, but the prep-coaching state-title clue isolates Palmer specifically.
questions.more: He was later named a national prep coach of the year.
questions.source: Heisman.com (Carson Palmer named National Prep Coach of the Year); MaxPreps

questions.cat: College football · nickname paired with Mr. Inside
questions.q: Doc Blanchard and Glenn Davis, Army's back-to-back Heisman winners, shared a famous pair of nicknames. Together they were known as 'Mr. Inside' and what?
questions.detail: Blanchard, the power runner, was 'Mr. Inside,' and Davis, the breakaway speedster, was 'Mr. Outside' in Army's wartime backfield.
questions.why: The answer comes from a tiny matched-pair set; once 'Mr. Inside' is given, the complement is deducible but specific.
questions.more: The duo shared a 1945 Time magazine cover.
questions.source: Heisman.com (Glenn Davis); Bleacher Report (Mr. Inside and Mr. Outside)

questions.cat: College football · school of two-time Heisman runner-up Lero
questions.dimension: school of two-time Heisman runner-up Leroy Keyes
questions.q: Before quarterbacks took over the Heisman race, one school produced two finalists who each finished second to O.J. Simpson and never won. That repeat runner-up was Leroy Keyes, of which school?
questions.detail: Leroy Keyes of Purdue was a Heisman finalist twice in the late 1960s, finishing second to O.J. Simpson in 1968 without ever winning.
questions.why: The 'twice a runner-up, never won, behind O.J.' slicer narrows a walkable set of 1960s stars to Keyes and routes to Purdue.
questions.more: Keyes starred on both offense and defense for the Boilermakers.
questions.source: NCAA.com (Heisman winners and runners-up since 1935); SI (Notable Heisman Runners-Up)

questions.cat: College football · position of Nebraska's 1972 Heisman winne
questions.q: Among the very few non-quarterback, non-running-back Heisman winners, one in 1972 was a flashy pass-catcher and returner for Nebraska. What position did Johnny Rodgers play?
questions.detail: Johnny Rodgers of Nebraska won the 1972 Heisman as a wide receiver and return man, one of only a handful of pure receivers ever to win the award.
questions.why: The position set is short, and the reflex is to guess running back for an old-school skill star; the clue 'pass-catcher and returner' isolates the rarely chosen receiver answer.
questions.more: Only a few wide receivers have ever won the Heisman.
questions.source: Heisman.com (Johnny Rodgers); Notre Dame 125 (receivers who won the Heisman)

questions.cat: College football · original Heisman award name
questions.q: The trophy given to college football's best player is named for a famous coach. The very first one handed out, though, carried a completely different name. What was that original name?
questions.detail: The 1935 award was the DAC Trophy; it was renamed for John Heisman after he died in 1936.
questions.why: The reflex is 'Heisman,' but the inaugural 1935 prize predated that name.
questions.more: The first winner was Jay Berwanger of the University of Chicago.
questions.source: Heisman.com history; NOLA.com Heisman backstory

questions.cat: College football · first 'Death Valley' nickname
questions.q: Two famous college football stadiums both go by the nickname 'Death Valley.' One school used the name more than a decade before the other. Which school used it first?
questions.detail: Clemson's Memorial Stadium was called Death Valley around 1948; LSU's Tiger Stadium picked it up by 1959.
questions.why: LSU's version is louder in pop culture, so people guess LSU; Clemson's claim is older.
questions.more: A visiting coach coined Clemson's version because his teams kept losing there.
questions.source: HERO Sports trivia; LSU Reveille history

questions.cat: College football · Commander-in-Chief's Trophy wins leader
questions.q: Three U.S. service academies compete each year for one shared football trophy. Through 2025, which of the three had won it the most times?
questions.detail: Air Force holds the most Commander-in-Chief's Trophy wins (about 21), ahead of Navy and Army.
questions.why: Army-Navy gets all the attention, so people skip Air Force, which actually leads the three-way trophy.
questions.more: The trophy, named for the U.S. president, was first awarded in 1972.
questions.source: Wikipedia Commander-in-Chief's Trophy; AOL rivalry explainer

questions.cat: College football · rivalry game nickname (neutral-site)
questions.q: The annual Georgia-Florida football game in Jacksonville was long known by a colorful nickname before the schools dropped it. What was the game called?
questions.answer_subject: The World's Largest Outdoor Cocktail Party
questions.state: The World's Largest Outdoor Cocktail Party
questions.detail: A 1950s sportswriter coined it after a fan offered a cop a drink; the schools later distanced themselves from the name.
questions.why: Many fans know the game but blank on the official old nickname tied to its tailgate reputation.
questions.more: The schools cooled on the name after unruly 1980s crowds.
questions.source: VinePair Georgia-Florida history; WHNT SEC report

questions.cat: College football · oldest active college football stadium
questions.q: The NCAA recognizes one stadium as the oldest still used for college football. Skip the famous huge stadiums and name the school whose ground has hosted football since the 1890s.
questions.detail: Franklin Field opened in 1895 and is the NCAA-recognized oldest college football stadium in operation.
questions.why: People assume an old powerhouse; the answer is an Ivy League field, not a modern blue-blood.
questions.more: Franklin Field also debuted the first stadium scoreboard.
questions.source: Penn Today 125 years of Franklin Field; ESPN oldest stadiums

questions.cat: College football · rivalry trophy animal shape
questions.q: Minnesota and Iowa play each year for a bronze trophy shaped like an animal. What animal is it?
questions.detail: Floyd of Rosedale is a bronze pig, born from a 1935 wager between the two states' governors over a real hog.
questions.why: Most fans know the trophy has a name but can't picture that it's modeled on a live prize pig.
questions.more: The original live pig died of cholera before the bronze version was cast.
questions.source: Gopher Sports Floyd of Rosedale; HawkCentral history

questions.cat: College football · rivalry trophy object type
questions.q: Notre Dame and USC play every year for a trophy that is studded with gems added after each game. What kind of object is this jeweled trophy?
questions.detail: The Jeweled Shillelagh gains a ruby Trojan head for USC wins and an emerald shamrock for Notre Dame wins.
questions.why: Fans recall it sparkles but often can't name that it's an Irish fighting club, not a cup or bell.
questions.more: The original was retired in 1995 and a longer replacement made.
questions.source: USC Athletics Jeweled Shillelagh; ND WNDU feature

questions.cat: College football · live-animal field-entry tradition
questions.q: At Colorado home games, a live animal is released to thunder onto the field ahead of the team. What animal makes that famous run?
questions.detail: Colorado's live buffalo, named Ralphie, has galloped the team onto the field since the 1960s.
questions.why: People picture a costumed mascot; here it is a genuine 700-pound bison sprinting across the turf.
questions.more: The first Ralphie debuted in 1966; each successor keeps the name.
questions.source: ESPN Ralphie VII debut; CU Buffs athletics

questions.cat: College football · scoring-celebration vehicle mascot
questions.q: Oklahoma celebrates every score by sending a small covered wagon racing across the field, pulled by two ponies. What is that wagon called?
questions.detail: The Conestoga-style wagon, pulled by ponies Boomer and Sooner, crosses the field after Oklahoma scores.
questions.why: Fans know the 'Boomer Sooner' chant but not the wagon's specific name or that real ponies pull it.
questions.more: It was introduced in 1964 and became the official mascot in 1980.
questions.source: Wikipedia Sooner Schooner; OU Sooner Traditions

questions.cat: College football · Script Ohio i-dotter instrument
questions.q: Ohio State's marching band spells out a famous script word on the field, ending with one player 'dotting the i.' Which instrument does that honored player carry?
questions.detail: Since 1937 a senior sousaphone player stands as the dot of the 'i' in Script Ohio.
questions.why: People assume the dotter plays something small; it switched to the band's biggest horn for visual effect.
questions.more: The very first i-dotter actually played a cornet before the switch.
questions.source: Wikipedia Script Ohio; OSU Marching Band traditions

questions.cat: College football · rivalry trophy name vs. game nickname
questions.dimension: rivalry trophy name vs. game nickname
questions.q: Mississippi State and Ole Miss play the 'Egg Bowl,' but the trophy itself predates that nickname by about half a century. What is the trophy called?
questions.detail: The football-shaped Golden Egg has been awarded since 1927; the 'Egg Bowl' nickname only arrived in 1979.
questions.why: The catchy 'Egg Bowl' name hides that the actual trophy has a different, older name.
questions.more: The trophy was created to calm fan violence after an early game.
questions.source: Wikipedia Egg Bowl; SI Egg Bowl history

questions.cat: College football · longest uninterrupted intersectional seri
questions.q: The longest uninterrupted yearly series between two schools from different regions ran 93 straight seasons until 2020. Which independent program was at the center of it?
questions.detail: Notre Dame and Navy played every year from 1927 through 2019 before COVID-19 ended the streak in 2020.
questions.why: People think of conference rivalries; this is a coast-spanning series tied to a WWII-era debt of gratitude.
questions.more: Notre Dame once won 43 straight in the series, an FBS record for one matchup.
questions.source: Wikipedia Navy-Notre Dame rivalry; Fighting Irish series history

questions.cat: College football · shared bowl debut year
questions.q: Counting only bowl games that have been played continuously since they began, three different bowls all debuted on the very same New Year's Day. In what year did that trio first kick off?
questions.detail: The Orange, Sugar, and Sun bowls all first played on January 1, 1935.
questions.why: The Rose Bowl is older but had a long gap; this slicer is the cluster of three that launched together.
questions.more: The Cotton Bowl followed two years later in 1937.
questions.source: Oldest.org college bowl games; BroBible oldest bowls

questions.cat: College football · rivalry trophy household-object type
questions.q: Purdue and Indiana have handed a wooden trophy to the rivalry winner every year since the 1920s. What everyday object is the trophy?
questions.detail: First awarded in 1925, the Old Oaken Bucket gains a bronze 'P' or 'I' link after each game.
questions.why: Many recall a Big Ten trophy but not that it's literally an old farm water bucket.
questions.more: It was found on a southern Indiana farm by representatives of both schools.
questions.source: Purdue Sports trophy rivalries; Wikipedia Old Oaken Bucket

questions.cat: College football · lumberjack-themed rivalry trophy
questions.q: Wisconsin and Minnesota play for a giant trophy modeled on a tool used by a tall-tale lumberjack. What is the trophy?
questions.detail: The winner takes Paul Bunyan's Axe, a six-foot-handled axe engraved with every game's score.
questions.why: Several Big Ten trophies sound similar; this slicer pins the folklore axe to this specific border rivalry.
questions.more: It replaced an earlier 'Slab of Bacon' trophy that went missing in 1943.
questions.source: Gopher Sports Paul Bunyan's Axe; Wikipedia Minnesota-Wisconsin rivalry

questions.cat: College football · Army-Navy host city
questions.q: For most of the 20th century, the Army-Navy game was hosted at a neutral site chosen because it sits roughly between the two academies. Which city held it for the most games?
questions.detail: Philadelphia, between West Point and Annapolis, has hosted the large majority of Army-Navy games since 1899.
questions.why: People guess Washington or New York; the midpoint logic points to Philadelphia.
questions.more: Early games were at Penn's Franklin Field before later stadiums.
questions.source: Visit Philly Army-Navy; PhillyLovesArmyNavy rivalry history

questions.cat: College football · second-largest stadium capacity (Big Ten
questions.q: Of the four well-known Big Ten 'horseshoe' giants, which school's stadium edges out Ohio State's for the second-largest capacity, sitting just behind Michigan?
questions.detail: Beaver Stadium (about 106,500) is second only to Michigan Stadium (about 107,600).
questions.why: People assume Ohio Stadium is No. 2; Beaver Stadium actually edges it out by several thousand seats.
questions.more: Ohio Stadium sits third in this group, just above Texas A&M's Kyle Field.
questions.source: Front Office Sports biggest stadiums; ESPN biggest stadiums

questions.cat: College football · stadium nickname crediting a coach
questions.q: Notre Dame's home stadium earned a nickname crediting the coach who oversaw its 1930 construction. By what name is the stadium known?
questions.detail: Notre Dame Stadium opened in 1930 under Knute Rockne and carries his name in its nickname.
questions.why: The Yankee Stadium 'House That Ruth Built' echo trips people; this is the football version tied to Rockne.
questions.more: Its original capacity was about 59,000.
questions.source: Notre Dame 125 Football moments; Wikipedia Notre Dame Stadium

questions.cat: College football · named SEC mega-stadium
questions.q: Among the four service-academy and Big Ten 'horseshoe' giants compared by capacity, Texas A&M's home stadium ranks near the top. What is that stadium called?
questions.detail: Kyle Field seats about 102,700, ranking among the very largest in the country.
questions.why: Fans know A&M draws huge crowds but often can't name the stadium itself.
questions.more: It sits just behind Ohio Stadium in capacity among the biggest venues.
questions.source: Front Office Sports biggest stadiums; ESPN biggest stadiums

questions.cat: College football · founding-group bowl identity
questions.q: Set the Rose Bowl aside. Of the bowls that have been played without interruption, the oldest among the trio that launched together carries the name of a Southern city. Which bowl, played in Miami, is part of that founding group?
questions.detail: The Orange Bowl, in Miami, debuted January 1, 1935 alongside the Sugar and Sun bowls.
questions.why: Asked to skip the Rose Bowl, people grope for the next-oldest; the Miami clue isolates the Orange Bowl.
questions.more: All three of that trio still play today.
questions.source: Oldest.org college bowl games; Britannica Rose Bowl

questions.cat: College football · most famous fight song
questions.q: One sousaphone-and-script tradition aside, name the marching band whose 'Victory March' is widely ranked the most famous fight song in college football.
questions.detail: Notre Dame's 'Victory March,' copyrighted in 1908, is routinely ranked the best-known college fight song.
questions.why: Many strong fight songs compete, but rankings consistently put this one first.
questions.more: It gained extra fame from the 1993 film 'Rudy.'
questions.source: Notre Dame News fight-song ranking; SI greatest fight songs

questions.cat: College football · rivalry trophy original finish
questions.q: The trophy for the Texas-Oklahoma game is a ten-gallon cowboy hat, but it wasn't always gold. When it was first made in 1941, what metal color was it?
questions.detail: The Red River trophy began as the Bronze Hat in 1941 and was refinished gold in the 1970s.
questions.why: Everyone knows it as the 'Golden Hat,' hiding that it started out bronze.
questions.more: The game also hands out additional trophies beyond the hat.
questions.source: Yahoo Sports Golden Hat explainer; Red River Rivalry traditions
